content:
New Blood or Old Guard? Supplier Churn at Ribble Valley

Key Findings

  • Ribble Valley Borough Council spent a total of £38.6M across 13,723 transactions between 2021/22 and 2025/26.
  • Overall spending increased by £6.0M, representing an increase of 111.8% from 2021 to 2025.
  • The largest annual spending increase occurred between 2024 and 2025, rising by £3.2M, a jump of 39.6%.
  • Over the five-year period, the council engaged with 1,282 unique suppliers.

Ribble Valley's Expanding Financial Footprint

Ribble Valley Borough Council's spending has seen a significant increase over the five financial years from 2021/22 to 2025/26. The total expenditure analysed by AI DOGE stands at £38.6M, spread across 13,723 individual transactions. This substantial sum highlights the scale of financial operations required to deliver public services within the borough.

An in-depth look at the annual figures reveals a consistent upward trend in spending. In 2021, the council spent £5.4M. This figure rose to £6.1M in 2022, marking an increase of £775K or 14.4%. The following year, 2023, saw spending reach £7.6M, an even larger jump of £1.4M or 23.1%. This pattern of growth indicates an expanding range of activities or increasing costs associated with existing services.

Analysing Spending Growth and Supplier Engagement

The upward trajectory of spending continued into 2024, with the council disbursing £8.1M, an increase of £580K or 7.7% from the previous year. However, the most pronounced increase occurred between 2024 and 2025, when spending surged by £3.2M to reach £11.4M. This represents a substantial annual growth of 39.6%, the largest percentage increase within the analysed period. Overall, the council's spending more than doubled, increasing by £6.0M or 111.8% from 2021 to 2025.

Over these five years, Ribble Valley Borough Council engaged with a considerable number of different businesses and organisations. The data shows that a total of 1,282 unique suppliers received payments from the council. This high number of individual suppliers, relative to the 13,723 transactions, suggests a diverse procurement landscape. It implies that many transactions may be with one-off or infrequent vendors, rather than a small core group of long-term partners, though the specific retention rates are not detailed in the available data.

Implications of a Diverse Supplier Base

Managing a supplier base of 1,282 unique entities over five years presents both opportunities and challenges for the council. A broad range of suppliers can foster competition, potentially leading to better value for money and access to specialised services. It also supports local economies by distributing contracts among many businesses. However, it also requires robust procurement processes and administrative oversight to ensure compliance, performance, and financial accountability for each of the 13,723 transactions.

The significant increase in overall spending, particularly the £3.2M rise in the final year, underscores the importance of transparency in public procurement. As the council's financial commitments grow, understanding how these funds are allocated across its extensive supplier network becomes even more critical. Publicly available data, such as that analysed by AI DOGE, allows citizens to scrutinise these expenditures and hold their local authority accountable for its financial decisions and supplier relationships.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to Ribble Valley Borough Council about their supplier engagement strategies or procurement policies.
  • Attend council committee meetings, as they are open to the public, to hear discussions on financial matters and service delivery.
  • Check the council's published contracts register to see details of larger contracts awarded to suppliers.
  • Write to your local councillor about the council's approach to managing its large supplier base and ensuring value for money.
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data for Ribble Valley Borough Council and other Lancashire councils yourself.
This article is based on Ribble Valley Borough Council's published spending data covering 2021/22 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
